quote: The body is not an instrument of the mind; it is the living subject.
through_line: Thursday goes spare and wide. The Aeon piece on embodiment is the week’s most quietly radical argument — that being human isn’t primarily about cognition but about the whole animal: the gut, the skin, the proprioceptive sense of where you are in space. Impermanence arrives as a companion, asking what it means to see this clearly without despair. The year’s mathematics and the search data run alongside, not competing with the philosophical weight but holding a different kind of clarity.

articles.title: To be fully human, we must also be fully embodied animal
articles.pitch: A philosophical essay arguing that the reduction of human identity to cognitive capacity — the version of personhood that AI development implicitly promotes — leaves out most of what it actually means to be alive. Drawing on phenomenology, evolutionary biology, and embodied cognition, the author makes the case that our animality is not incidental but constitutive. One of the week’s harder pieces, and worth it.

articles.title: AI Search Statistics 2026
articles.pitch: A current aggregation of AI search adoption statistics for 2026 — market share, growth rates, query volumes, and demographic patterns drawn from multiple data sources. Not a think piece, but a useful reference document for anyone who needs numbers to anchor their thinking about where search is actually going.

articles.title: The Insight of Impermanence
articles.pitch: A short dharma piece that distinguishes between knowing intellectually that everything changes and actually perceiving impermanence as it happens — and asks what shifts when you can do the latter. The writing is careful without being academic; the kind of piece that rewards returning to over time.

articles.title: The Year in Mathematics
articles.pitch: Quanta’s annual mathematics retrospective covers the year’s most significant results across number theory, topology, probability, and beyond. What makes it worth reading beyond interest in the problems is the underlying story of how mathematical insight actually moves — in fits and starts, across disciplines, through unexpected connections.
